title: Security Engineering Inside AI Pipelines

modules.title: Module 1: Where AI Pipelines Break — Supply Chain, Training Data, and Deployment Threats
modules.sub: Mapping the unique attack surfaces AI development introduces
modules.description: Learners survey the end-to-end AI development lifecycle and pinpoint where conventional DevSecOps controls fall short. They examine real-world compromise scenarios spanning poisoned training data, malicious dependencies, and tampered model artifacts to build a mental model of AI-specific risk. By the end, learners can articulate why traditional security frameworks need augmentation before being applied to ML systems.

modules.title: Module 2: Threat Modeling for ML Systems — STRIDE Applied to AI Workflows
modules.sub: Adapting STRIDE to expose threats across machine learning workflows
modules.description: Learners apply the STRIDE framework to a representative ML pipeline, systematically identifying spoofing, tampering, repudiation, information disclosure, denial-of-service, and elevation-of-privilege risks at each stage. They work through structured exercises to produce a threat model diagram annotated with AI-specific threat actors and attack vectors. The result is a reusable threat modeling methodology they can carry into their own organizations.

modules.title: Module 3: Securing Data Ingestion, Labeling, and Model Training Environments
modules.sub: Hardening the earliest and most vulnerable stages of ML development
modules.description: Learners evaluate the security posture of data ingestion pipelines, third-party labeling workflows, and training infrastructure, then implement controls to close identified gaps. They configure integrity checks for incoming datasets, review access policies for labeling platforms, and apply environment hardening techniques to training clusters. Decisions focus on balancing security rigor with the operational realities of data-heavy ML workloads.

modules.title: Module 4: Vulnerability Scanning and SAST for AI-Adjacent Code and Dependencies
modules.sub: Finding exploitable weaknesses in ML codebases and their dependency chains
modules.description: Learners run static application security testing tools against ML framework code, custom training scripts, and third-party library manifests to surface vulnerabilities before they reach production. They interpret scan output, triage findings by exploitability and blast radius, and determine appropriate remediation paths for AI-specific dependencies like model serialization libraries. The module builds confidence in applying existing AppSec tooling to the nuances of ML codebases.

modules.title: Module 5: Model Signing, Provenance Tracking, and Artifact Integrity
modules.sub: Ensuring every model artifact is verified, traceable, and tamper-evident
modules.description: Learners implement cryptographic signing workflows for trained model artifacts and establish provenance records that link each model version to its training data, code, and environment. They configure a model registry to enforce signature verification before any artifact can progress through the pipeline, closing off unsigned or unverified model injection attacks. By the end, they can demonstrate a full chain of custody from raw data to a deployable model file.

modules.title: Module 6: Runtime Monitoring — Detecting Inference Attacks and Model Drift in Production
modules.sub: Observing live model behavior to catch adversarial inputs and degradation
modules.description: Learners instrument a deployed model endpoint to capture inference telemetry, then configure detection rules targeting adversarial input patterns, anomalous query volumes, and statistical drift in model outputs. They analyze simulated attack logs to distinguish benign distribution shift from active adversarial probing and tune alert thresholds accordingly. The module produces a monitoring runbook that security and ML operations teams can jointly own.

modules.title: Module 7: Building Security Gates into CI/CD Pipelines for AI Releases
modules.sub: Automating security enforcement across every AI deployment stage
modules.description: Learners design and implement security gates within a CI/CD pipeline that block AI releases failing artifact integrity checks, dependency scans, or policy-defined risk thresholds. They construct pipeline-as-code configurations that integrate model signing verification, SAST results, and provenance attestations as mandatory pre-deployment conditions. The capstone exercise produces a reference pipeline template learners can adapt for their own AI release workflows.

synopsis: A compromised model training pipeline can silently corrupt an AI system's behavior at scale — and most DevSecOps practices were designed for traditional software, leaving critical gaps. This course teaches security engineers and developers exactly where AI-specific attack surfaces emerge in the development lifecycle and how to close them.
rationale: As organizations ship AI features through automated pipelines, attackers are targeting training data, model registries, and inference endpoints that fall outside conventional AppSec scope.
learning_outcome: Learners can threat model an AI pipeline, identify its highest-risk components, and implement concrete security gates from data ingestion through production deployment.
source_signals: NIST Releases New Control Overlays to Manage Cybersecurity Risks in AI Systems
